content:
We Gave Fictional Universes Real Memory — Here’s What Happened

6 min read
Just now

--

Building CogRealm: a knowledge-graph-powered app where Naruto can actually comfort Harry Potter, Tony Stark gives startup advice, and every answer is grounded in a real graph, not vibes or guessing.

Press enter or click to view image in full size

Overview of CogVerse
The idea
Somewhere between a hackathon prompt and a late-night “wait, what if — “ moment, this question showed up:
What if fictional characters didn’t just sound like themselves, but actually remembered their world — and could reason across worlds they were never written to meet? or you can build your own universe and simulate how a character would react in a certain situation.
Not “an LLM roleplaying a character.” Something closer to giving each fictional universe an actual memory: its characters, relationships, events, locations, objects — all as a real, queryable graph. Then let a language model speak through that graph instead of instead of it.
That became CogRealm, built for the WeMakeDevs Cognee Hackathon.
Ask it something like:
“What would happen if Harry Potter joins Slytherin?”
“Give me startup advice as Tony Stark. Here’s what my start up does…”
“Could Batman solve the Kira case?”
It’s grounded in facts actually extracted into a knowledge graph — and you can see exactly which facts, for every single response.
Nodes Extraction Example (Note: only 6 nodes are shown but there are plenty)Why “just prompt an LLM to roleplay” isn’t good enough
The obvious lazy version of this idea is a system prompt: “You are Tony Stark. Respond in character.” It works, sort of, for about two exchanges — and then it drifts, contradicts canon, or produces something that sounds like Tony Stark cosplaying as a motivational speaker.
Moreover, it only knows facts and relationships to an extent, so if you ask something more factual and character’s personality oriented, your typical LLMs will not be able to give a decent response.
The harder, more interesting version asks: what if the character’s personality, relationships, and history lived in an actual graph, and the LLM’s only job was to give that graph a voice?
That’s the architectural bet CogRealm makes.
UNSTRUCTURED DATA COGNITIVE GRAPH (COGNEE) GROUNDED GENERATION
┌───────────────────────────┐ ┌────────────────────────────────┐ ┌───────────────────┐
│ Walter_White.txt │ │ [Walter] --(allies)--> [Jesse] │ │ Groq LLM Engine │
│ Sirius_Black_Death.txt ├─────────►│ | │─────────►│ System Prompt │
│ Inosuke_Hashibira.txt │ Ingest │ (values) │ Retrieve │ + Graph Context │
└───────────────────────────┘ │ ▼ │ └─────────┬─────────┘
│ [Control] │ │
└────────────────────────────────┘ ▼
In-Character ResponseHow it actually works
Every fictional universe — MCU, Harry Potter, Naruto, Breaking Bad, Kung Fu Panda — gets its own dataset inside Cognee. Wiki content gets rewritten into clean declarative sentences (Cognee’s entity extraction is dramatically better at “Sirius Black is Harry Potter’s godfather” than at raw wiki infobox markup), then ingested via Cognee’s add() → cognify() pipeline, which builds an actual knowledge graph: entities, relationships, events, all connected.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Example of a Memory Document of Tony StarkWhen you ask a character something, the FastAPI backend runs a GRAPH_COMPLETION search against that universe's graph, pulls back the facts genuinely relevant to your question, and only then hands them to Groq (running gpt-oss-120b) along with a persona prompt to generate the in-character voice.
Cognee decides what’s true. Groq decides how it’s said. Neither one does the other’s job and that’s how we can get a quality result.
The cross-universe part (this is the fun bit)
The real differentiator isn’t single-character chat — plenty of projects do that. It’s asking a character to reason about a world they were never written to know about.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Cross Universe ExampleUnder the hood, this fires two separate graph queries — one against the speaking character’s own universe for their personality and voice, one against the referenced universe for the actual event or person being asked about — and merges both into a single persona prompt before Groq ever sees it.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Response of CogVerse to the above QueryProving it’s not just RAG with extra steps
Anyone can claim “we used a knowledge graph.” The harder problem is making that visible, not just true. Two features exist specifically for this:
The Memory Trace — every response has a collapsible panel underneath it showing the exact facts retrieved from the graph that informed that specific answer. No hidden context, no trust-me-it’s-grounded.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Example of Memory TraceThe Relationship Graph — click “View memory graph” on any character and you get a live radial visualization of their actual extracted relationships, pulled straight from Cognee’s graph via GRAPH_COMPLETION with only_context=True and parsed from the raw connection triples. Adjustable node count, colored to match the universe's theme.
Memory Graph View of Extracted RelationshipsThemes
We also added some custom logos of “CogVerse” for every universe we have added so far.

We have also added personlized “suggested questions” for every character so that users can click on them if they don’t have any ideas on what to ask.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Examples of personalized questions for various charactersTech Architecture
Below is the conceptual architecture of how CogRealm ingests lore data, links characters, processes chat queries via graph retrieval, and synthesizes grounded responses.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Cross-Universe GatewayThe parts that didn’t go smoothly (because nothing ever does)
This is usually the part hackathon writeups skip, and it’s the part worth including:

• Cognee’s default install quietly stores its database inside your virtual-env — meaning a routine venv rebuild would have silently wiped an entire ingested graph. Caught it early, moved storage outside the venv.

• Migrating to Cognee Cloud to actually use the hackathon’s sponsored credits hit a genuine server-side TLS handshake failure — confirmed via curl, raw openssl, and Python, across two machines and a mobile hotspot, before concluding it then finding out we were supposed to add API_Base_URL for setting connection. 😅
None of these were exotic. All of them were the kind of thing you only find by actually shipping. Had fun while solving these errors.
What’s next

• Automatic cross-universe intent detection, instead of manually picking the referenced universe from a dropdown

• Feature which allows users to create there own universes easily from the web interface itself.
Try it yourself
[IMAGE: A final wide shot of the full interface — sidebar with a few universes expanded showing their themed logos, main chat panel open — as a closing “here’s the whole thing” visual.]

Built for the WeMakeDevs Cognee Hackathon. Powered by Cognee for graph memory and Groq for inference.
